column2: In minimal change type IgA nephropathy, the whole slide image typically shows normal or near-normal glomeruli with no significant cellular proliferation or hypercellularity. The tubules appear normal or may show slight atrophy, and there is usually no significant interstitial fibrosis or inflammation. The vasculature appears normal. The hallmark feature is the presence of immunoglobulin A (IgA) deposits in the mesangial areas, which may be diffuse or patchy and are often not easily visible on H&E stains but can be detected by immunofluorescence. Electron microscopy would reveal characteristic subepithelial dense deposits beneath the visceral epithelial cells. Overall, the histological appearance is generally unremarkable without specific staining techniques to detect the IgA deposits.

In minimal change type IgA nephropathy, the whole slide image typically shows normal or near-normal glomeruli with no significant cellular proliferation or hypercellularity. The tubules appear normal or may show slight atrophy, and there is often no or minimal interstitial fibrosis. The vasculature appears unremarkable. The hallmark feature is the presence of immunoglobulin A (IgA) deposits in the mesangial areas, which may be diffuse or patchy, and these deposits are usually not as prominent or extensive as those seen in other types of IgA nephropathy. Electron microscopy would typically reveal subepithelial dense deposits, but this is not visible in the whole slide image. Overall, the histology in minimal change type IgA nephropathy lacks the characteristic features that would suggest more severe or typical forms of IgA nephropathy.

In minimal change type IgA nephropathy, the whole slide image typically shows normal or near-normal glomeruli with no significant cellular proliferation or hypercellularity. The tubules appear normal or may show slight atrophy, and there is often no or mild interstitial fibrosis. The vasculature appears unremarkable. The hallmark feature is the absence of characteristic histological changes such as mesangial hypercellularity, endocapillary hypercellularity, or segmental sclerosis. Instead, the image may reveal mild mesangial widening or mild mesangial hypercellularity, which can be subtle and not always apparent. Immunohistochemistry for IgA may show patchy or focal deposition in the mesangium, but this finding is not specific to minimal change type IgA nephropathy and can be variable. Overall, the appearance is generally unremarkable or minimally abnormal, making the diagnosis challenging without clinical context.

In minimal change type IgA nephropathy, the whole slide image typically shows normal or near-normal glomeruli with no significant cellular proliferation or hypercellularity. The tubules appear normal or may show slight atrophy, and there is usually no significant interstitial fibrosis or inflammation. The vasculature appears unremarkable. The hallmark feature is the presence of immunoglobulin A (IgA) deposits in the mesangial areas, which may be diffuse or patchy, often appearing as granular or punctate staining on the image. These deposits are usually not as prominent or extensive as those seen in other forms of IgA nephropathy. There is often a marked absence of cellular crescents and no significant basement membrane thickening or other structural abnormalities that might be seen in more severe forms of the disease.

The tubules appear normal or may show slight atrophy, and there is often no or mild interstitial fibrosis. The vasculature appears unremarkable. The hallmark feature is the presence of immunoglobulin A (IgA) deposits in the mesangial areas, which may be diffuse or patchy, and these deposits are usually not as prominent or extensive as those seen in other types of IgA nephropathy. Electron microscopy would typically reveal subepithelial dense deposits, but this is not visible in routine H&E stained whole slide images. There is also a lack of significant crescent formation or other signs of acute injury. Overall, the histological appearance is generally unremarkable or minimally abnormal, which can make the diagnosis challenging without specialized stains or electron microscopy.
